topic: The British Experience of Warfare c1790-1918
subject: history

items.q: Why did the Crimean War lead to army reform?
items.correct: Disasters in supply, medicine and command, widely reported in the press, showed the army was poorly organised and forced change.
items.misconception: The Crimean War was a smooth British triumph with no problems.
items.random_wrong: The Crimean War was fought in South Africa.
items.why: Students assume British victories were always efficient; the Crimea's mismanagement, publicised by war correspondents, drove demands for reform.

items.q: Did Britain enter WWI with a fully modern mass army?
items.correct: Britain relied on a small professional force and volunteers in 1914, only introducing conscription in 1916 as casualties mounted.
items.misconception: Britain had a large conscript army ready from the start of WWI.
items.random_wrong: Britain never used conscription in WWI.
items.why: Learners assume mass conscription existed from 1914; Britain initially depended on volunteers and the regular army before compulsion.

items.q: Were army reforms like Cardwell's immediately effective?
items.correct: They modernised structure but weaknesses persisted, as the Boer War later exposed, prompting the further Haldane reforms.
items.misconception: One set of reforms instantly perfected the British army.
items.random_wrong: The Cardwell Reforms created the Royal Air Force.
items.why: Students expect single reforms to fix everything; military modernisation came in stages across decades in response to repeated failures.

items.q: Which 1854-56 war exposed major failings in the British army's supply and medical care?
items.why: It's The Crimean War. “The Boer War” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What did the Cardwell Reforms abolish?
items.why: It's The purchase of officer commissions. “The Royal Navy” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What characterised fighting on the Western Front in WWI?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Static trench warfare. “Rapid cavalry charges” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What did the 1916 Military Service Act introduce?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Compulsory conscription. “A volunteer-only army” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was conscription introduced in Britain during WWI?
items.why: The real figure is 1916 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
